Summary:
Relationship structure: causal chain.

OLIG2 is a master regulator required for oligodendrocyte lineage specification and for later stages of oligodendrocyte differentiation in vivo in mouse across NSCs, OPCs, and NFOs. Mechanistically, OLIG2 binds genomic DNA to directly activate or repress many protein-coding genes and >600 expressed lncRNAs (some lncRNAs are OLIG2-repressed in NSCs and others OLIG2-activated in OPCs/NFOs), occupies promoter regions of dynamically regulated bivalent genes (H3K4me3/H3K27me3), and likely cooperates with other TFs at shared motifs. Separately, OLIG2 binds the AU-rich 3′ UTR of its own Olig2 mRNA and, in reporter and competition experiments, can inhibit reporter expression via that 3′ UTR and influence endogenous OLIG2 protein levels, indicating self-regulation after transcription. Outcomes include modulation of OPC formation, promotion of oligodendrocyte differentiation, and repression of neuronal programs.
References: Wei, H., Dong, X., You, Y., Hai, B., Duran, R. C., Wu, X., Kharas, N., & Wu, J. Q. (2021). OLIG2 regulates lncRNAs and its own expression during oligodendrocyte lineage formation. BMC Biol, 19(1), 132. https://doi.org/10.1186/s12915-021-01057-6
